original_abstract: The proliferation of lithium microstructures during electrochemical cycling remains a critical barrier to the commercialization of high-energy-density Li-metal batteries. In this study, in situ $^7$Li magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is employed to investigate the relationship between electrolyte concentration dynamics and the morphology of lithium deposition in symmetric cells. By utilizing chemical shift imaging (CSI), we distinguish between different deposition environments, specifically separating mossy lithium growth from runaway dendritic structures based on their unique bulk magnetic susceptibility signatures. Our results demonstrate that mossy structures begin to form almost immediately upon charging across all tested current densities. However, the transition to rapid dendritic growth occurs at a distinct onset time. At high current densities, this onset correlates strongly with the depletion of lithium ions near the electrode surface, consistent with the theoretical Sand’s time model. In contrast, at low current densities, dendrite initiation occurs significantly earlier than predicted by mass-transport limitations, suggesting that alternative mechanisms—such as those driven by surface inhomogeneities or solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) instability—dominate the low-current regime. These findings provide a robust experimental framework for quantifying the spatial and temporal evolution of lithium microstructures, offering insights essential for the development of strategies to mitigate dendrite formation.
humanized_abstract: The growth of lithium microstructures during cycling remains a major obstacle towards the successful commercialization of Li-metal batteries with high energy density. Using in situ 7Li MRI, in this study we elucidate the correlation between the dynamics of the electrolyte composition and the morphological characteristics of lithium formed in Li symmetric cells, by employing CSI to differentiate between various environments (deposited lithium at different time, etc. based on their distinct bulk magnetic susceptibility); namely between the mossy lithium deposits and the undesirable and detrimental runoff dendrites. It is demonstrated for the first time that in all investigated current densities mossy lithium structures start to form within a few seconds upon charging; while their transition into the undesirable runoff dendrites occur at a specific onset time, occurring at the Sand’s time at high current densities; while at low current densities the onset time of such dendrites’ formation occurs much earlier than the time estimated from the mass transport considerations, indicating the effect of other less dominant sources at low current densities such as: a) the surface inhomogeneities on the electrolyte/ electrode surface, b) instability in the SEI layer; thus bridging the gap towards understanding various sources of lithium microstructure formations, enabling a robust experimental understanding of their formations.

It is demonstrated for the first time that in all investigated current densities mossy lithium structures start to form within a few seconds upon charging; while their transition into the undesirable runoff dendrites occur at a specific onset time, occurring at the Sand’s time at high current densities; while at low current densities the onset time of such dendrites’ formation occurs much earlier than the time estimated from the mass transport considerations, indicating the effect of other less dominant sources at low current densities such as: a) the surface inhomogeneities on the electrolyte/ electrode surface, b) instability in the SEI layer; thus bridging the gap towards understanding various sources of lithium microstructure formations, enabling a robust experimental understanding of their formations.
